evidence: The statement defines F(n) via regular induced subgraphs: 'every graph on n vertices contains a regular induced subgraph on at least F(n) vertices'.
verify_note: decl Erdos82.erdos_82 exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; regular induced subgraph is the defining object of F(n)

evidence: IsRegularInduced requires the induced subgraph to be regular of some degree k, the load-bearing property in F(n)/log n -> infinity.
verify_note: decl exists with research attribute; QID live, label exact; regularity (IsRegularOfDegree) is the load-bearing property of the subgraph

evidence: The problem asks whether g(n) = #{m : phi(m) = n}, the multiplicity of Euler's totient function, exceeds n^{1-eps} infinitely often.
verify_note: decl Erdos821.erdos_821 exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; g(n)=#{m: totient m=n} makes phi central

evidence: The statement asks whether the set of integers of the form n + phi(n) has positive density, with phi Euler's totient function (Nat.totient).
verify_note: decl Erdos822.erdos_822 exists @[category research solved]; QID live, label exact; statement is about range of n+Nat.totient n

evidence: The question is whether the range of n + phi(n) 'has positive (lower) density' (Set.HasPosDensity).
verify_note: decl exists; QID live, label exact; HasPosDensity ('positive (lower) density') is the question itself

evidence: The hypothesis sigma(n) > Cn uses the sum-of-divisors function (ArithmeticFunction.sigma 1) and asks when n is a distinct sum of its proper divisors.
verify_note: decl Erdos825.erdos_825 exists @[category research solved]; QID live, label exact; hypothesis sigma 1 n > C*n is the sum-of-divisors function

evidence: The statement bounds tau(n + k) = sigma 0 (n + k), the number-of-divisors function, by O(k) for all k >= 1.
verify_note: decl Erdos826.erdos_826 exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; sigma 0 (n+k) = tau, the divisor-counting function, is the bounded quantity

evidence: The problem asks whether for every integer a there are infinitely many n with phi(n) | n + a, where phi is Euler's totient function.
verify_note: decl Erdos828.erdos_828 exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; phi(n) | n+a is the whole statement

evidence: The docstring states 'Lehmer conjectured that phi(n) | n - 1 if and only if n is prime', which is exactly Lehmer's totient problem.
verify_note: decl Erdos828.erdos_828.variants.lehmer_conjecture exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; decl states phi(n)|n-1 iff n prime for n>1, which IS Lehmer's totient problem

evidence: The problem asks whether the number of representations of n as a sum of two cubes ((1_A * 1_A)(n) for A the perfect cubes) is polylogarithmic.
verify_note: decl Erdos829.erdos_829 exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; sumRep cubes n counts representations as a sum of two cubes, the central quantity

evidence: Part 1 asks 'Are there infinitely many amicable pairs?', with IsAmicable a b defined by sigma(a) = sigma(b) = a + b.
verify_note: decl Erdos830.erdos_830.parts.i exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact ('amicable pair' is an alias); IsAmicable is the statement's subject

evidence: Part 2 asks whether A(x), the count of amicable pairs 1 <= a <= b <= x, satisfies A(x) > x^{1-o(1)}.
verify_note: decl Erdos830.erdos_830.parts.ii exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; A(x) counts amicable pairs

evidence: The variant docstring restates the problem as 'whether there exists k > 2 such that the chromatic number of the Johnson graph J(2k, k) is k+1'.
verify_note: decl Erdos835.erdos_835.variants.johnson exists @[category research open]; QID live, label exact; J(2k,k) Johnson graph is the object of the variant

evidence: The statement asserts J(2*k, k).chromaticNumber = k + 1 for some k > 2, so the chromatic number is the central quantity.
verify_note: decl exists; QID live, label exact; chromaticNumber = k+1 is the asserted equality
